The athlete can follow it or ignore it — it is a guide, not a gate, and can run alone or alongside the bell.

┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  Wellness · 10:00        ● any   │
│                                 │
│            07:42                │   ← remaining sit time
│                                 │
│            ╭───────╮            │
│           (  inhale )           │   ← expanding-orb pacer, 6 bpm
│            ╰───────╯              │     (4s in / 6s out) — optional
│                                 │
│   next bell in 0:18  · every 2m │   ← interval bell
│                                 │
│  [ pacer: orb ▾ ]  [ bell ▾ ]   │   ← quick toggles; either can be off
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌────────────┐  │
│  │   PAUSE  │   │   DONE     │  │
│  └──────────┘   └────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
  • Defaults are unguided: no voice. If a guidedUrl is present it is offered as an optional link, never auto-played.
  • An optional script (e.g. breathing instruction) is shown as quiet text.
  • The breathing pacer is fully ignorable — a user who just wants the bell sees only the timer + chimes.

Acceptance criteria

  • A bell-only block runs a silent sit of durationMin with a chime every intervalMin, honoring startBell/endBell and optional haptics.
  • intervalMin is constrained to 1–5.
  • A breathing-only block runs the pacer at breathsPerMin with the configured inhaleSec/exhaleSec (and holds) in the chosen visual.
  • A block with both shows the pacer and chimes on the bell interval simultaneously.
  • The block is unguided by default; guidedUrl is only offered as a link and never auto-plays.
  • The breathing pacer can be toggled off; the user can complete the sit on the bell alone.
  • On RED, this block is offered as the session (with a true-rest-day alternative); it is never forced.
  • Completion writes a BlockResult with durationSec.

docs.title: 6. Post-session logging — quick "done + how it felt"
docs.text:
After the last block, a low-friction wrap-up captures done, an overall how-it-felt rating, and the key timer/strength results — then serializes the day's WorkoutLog (data-model.md §7). Everything was already written to IndexedDB as the session ran (§9); this screen is a confirm + a single felt rating, not a data-entry grind.

┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  Nice work.  58:00 · GREEN      │
│                                 │
│  How did it feel overall?       │
│   🙂1  🙂2  😐3  😕4  😣5        │   ← feltOverall 1–5, one tap
│                                 │
│  Captured automatically:        │
│   • DB press  4×8–10 logged     │
│   • AMRAP  6 + 8                │   ← from the timer (§4)
│   • Mobility + 10-min sit ✓     │
│                                 │
│  ▸ Add a note / flag a tweak    │   ← optional notes / PainFlag
│                                 │
│  ┌───────────────────────────┐  │
│  │      SAVE TO HISTORY      │  │
│  └───────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
  • feltOverall (1–5) is the one near-mandatory tap; everything else is pre-filled from per-block results.
  • Optional: a free-text notes, a PainFlag (joint + severity → the AI auto-cuts the offending volume on its next pass), or a benchmarkPR.
  • Save finalizes the WorkoutLog in the local store. The file is later committed to history/logs/ for the AI to read on its next /replan (the exact write-back path — direct file write vs. a Claude Code skill ingest of an in-app export — is an architecture.md §10 open question; the UX is the same regardless).

Acceptance criteria

  • The wrap-up is completable with a single tap (feltOverall); all other fields are optional and pre-filled.
  • Per-block results captured during the session (sets, timer score, durations) appear pre-filled and editable.
  • Saving writes a complete, schema-valid WorkoutLog to IndexedDB with no network dependency.
  • An optional PainFlag (joint + severity 1–5) and free-text notes can be attached.
  • tierUsed reflects any mid-session tier re-pick (§3.5).
  • The saved log is queued for serialization to history/logs/ (write-back mechanism per architecture §10); a failed/absent commit never loses the local record.

docs.title: 7. Display — both units, responsive phone ↔ iPad board
7.1 Both lb + kg, everywhere a load appears
docs.text: Loads and bodyweights are stored canonically in kg and rendered in both units at display time, leading with units.primary and showing the secondary alongside when units.showBoth is true (e.g. 50 lb / 22.5 kg). The athlete never converts mid-session. Display rounding respects units.loadRoundingKg.

docs.title: 7.2 Responsive: in-hand phone ↔ propped iPad gym board
docs.text:
The same web build and the same Session objects render at two sizes (architecture.md §4):

  • Phone, in-hand / in-pocket — the primary interactive surface: check-in, START, the runner, logging. Touch targets sized for sweaty hands.
  • Old iPad, propped as a gym display — the board view at /board/today: same data, big type, high contrast, legible across the room. The timer's huge countdown and the current movement + reps are the hero elements at distance.
  PHONE (in-hand)              iPad BOARD (across the room)
┌───────────────┐        ┌───────────────────────────────────┐
│ ● GREEN       │        │   AMRAP 8:00            04:12      │
│ Block A · D2  │        │                                   │
│ ~58m · 7 blk  │        │   ROW            12               │
│ [   START   ] │        │   DB THRUSTER     9   50 / 22.5    │
│ 🟢 🟡 🔴      │        │   round 6                         │
└───────────────┘        └───────────────────────────────────┘

Acceptance criteria

  • Every load/bodyweight renders in both lb and kg (leading with units.primary, secondary shown when showBoth), with no manual conversion anywhere.
  • Displayed loads honor units.loadRoundingKg.
  • The layout is legible in-hand on a phone (arm's length) and at a distance on a propped iPad; the timer's countdown and current movement scale up to be the hero elements on the board.
  • /board/today renders the same Session objects as the phone — the two cannot drift.
  • High-contrast rendering remains legible in a bright/garage-gym setting.

docs.title: 8. Chalkboard / print view — later, low priority
docs.text:
A chalkboard/print surface renders the same Session objects to a whiteboard-style card and (eventually) a PDF/PNG. The responsive board view (/board/today, §7) already covers the v1 "big display" need; a dedicated print/PDF export is deprioritized to later (architecture.md §9, §2 print row). It is mentioned here for completeness and intentionally not over-specified: same data, one more renderer, no second authoring system.

Acceptance criteria

  • The board view at /board/today exists in v1 and is the large-display surface.
  • A dedicated PDF/PNG print export is explicitly out of scope for v1 and, when built, reads the same Session objects (no new authoring path).

docs.title: 9. Offline-always guarantees
docs.text:
Everything in §§1–7 works with zero network, every time. This is non-negotiable (architecture.md §1).

  • Cached for offline: today's Session plus the next ~14 days are cached (PWA service worker); the app shell, the timer, and the engine are all local.
  • Local-first writes: ReadinessEntry, inline set logs, timer results, and the WorkoutLog are written to IndexedDB (Dexie) as they happen. No write waits on a round-trip.
  • Deterministic, offline readiness: the GREEN/AMBER/RED engine is a pure local function — START is instant with no LLM call (architecture.md §8).
  • Crash/reload safety: a mid-session reload loses nothing — on relaunch the runner restores the in-progress session: the current block index, completed sets, timer elapsed/round state, and the readiness tier are all rehydrated from the local store.

Acceptance criteria

  • With the network fully disabled, §§1–7 (home, check-in, runner, timer, meditation, logging, both-unit display) all function end to end.
  • Today + the next ~14 days of sessions are available offline.
  • All check-in, set-log, timer, and workout-log writes persist to IndexedDB without a network call.
  • A reload (or crash) mid-session restores block position, logged sets, timer state, and the active tier with no data loss.
  • The readiness tier is computed locally with zero network dependency.

docs.title: 10. End-to-end happy path — the 6am walkthrough
docs.text:
It's 6am. Box wifi is bad. The phone is in hand.

  1. Open the app. The home card (§1) renders instantly from the local store: "Block A · Day 2 — Press Emphasis + Sprinty Couplet, ~58 min," with a green accent from yesterday's plan. No network.
  2. One tap to confirm feel. Slept fine → tap 🟢 GREEN (§2). A wearable score field is there but ignorable; nothing synced, so the tap alone gates the day. (If it were day 6, a "still calibrating" note would remind the athlete the tap is what's trusted.) The engine computes computedTier = GREEN locally; the card stays green and the session loads as authored.
  3. Hit START (§1). The full-screen runner opens; the screen wake-lock engages (§3).
  4. Warm-up (§3.1): tick through the 13-minute temperature-raising ramp checklist.
  5. Strict skill + strength (§3.2): wall-HSPU negatives and seated DB press, 50 lb / 22.5 kg shown in both units; each set logged with a single ✓ as prescribed, with an optional rest timer between sets.
  6. Conditioning (§3.3 → §4): the timer takes over the screen — AMRAP 8:00. A 3-2-1 lead-in beeps, the huge countdown runs, ROW 12 / DB THRUSTER 9 sit in oversized type, loud beeps + phone buzz mark each round. On STOP, the result 6 rounds + 8 reps is captured.
  7. Mobility (§3.4, §3.6): per-side hip/hamstring holds count down, prompting a left→right switch.
  8. Meditation (§5): a 10-minute sit — the expanding-orb pacer breathes at 6/min while a chime lands every 2 minutes. (On a RED morning, this would have been the whole session — offered, not forced.)
  9. Wrap up (§6): "How did it feel?" → tap 2. The DB-press sets, the AMRAP score, and the mobility/sit durations are already filled. Save to history writes the WorkoutLog to IndexedDB.
  10. Mid-session reload, anytime: had the phone reloaded during the AMRAP, the runner would have restored the block position, logged sets, timer state, and the GREEN tier — nothing lost (§9).

The whole loop — open, one tap, START, train, log — ran fully offline, instant, with both units on every load and the human always one tap from overriding the tier.

docs.title: Nutrition — Fueling the Program
docs.text:
Fueling reference. This is the pillar that feeds the training: it sets a protein target, a way to fuel slow recomposition without obsessing, what to eat around morning sessions (including the espresso and the tendon pre-load), how to recover from a hard or restless night, and a short, honest supplement protocol. It is deliberately non-dogmatic and forkable — a set of defaults and principles, not a meal plan you must obey.

This doc is forkable and generic. It is written for "the athlete" / "you" so anyone can clone the repo and adapt it. The illustrative case running through it — a masters athlete who is an omnivore with no restrictions, wants protein-first guidance and to eyeball the rest, mixes home cooking with convenience food, drinks socially on weekends, and judges progress by the mirror and performance (not the scale) — is just an example that makes the defaults concrete. Swap in your own profile and the same logic holds.

Companion docs: science-foundations.md is the why (the evidence behind every number here — this doc cites it heavily); training-system.md is the coach (where these meals land in the day and the week); functional-design.md is the app UX; decisions.md is the log of athlete choices that set these defaults; and testing-protocol.md is the Week 1–2 baseline block. The guardrails the planner obeys live in CLAUDE.md.

A few ground rules, mirroring science-foundations.md:

  • Honesty about evidence strength is a feature. Each recommendation below is tagged [strong], [moderate], or [suggestive/mechanistic] so a forker can judge how hard to lean on it.
  • Protein-first, eyeball the rest. This whole doc is built so you hit one number reliably (protein) and steer everything else by simple defaults and the mirror — no macro logging required.
  • The scale is not the target. Recomposition (lose fat / gain muscle at once) routinely shows up in the mirror and in performance long before — or instead of — a moving bodyweight. We track those.

docs.title: 1. The one number that matters: protein
docs.text:
If you change nothing else, change this. Protein is the single highest-leverage nutrition variable for someone building upper-body muscle, recomposing, and recovering from hard training.

Target: ~1.6–2.2 g of protein per kg of bodyweight per day. [strong]

  • The classic meta-analysis places the point of diminishing returns for muscle gain at ~1.6 g/kg/day, with a safety ceiling around 2.2 g/kg/day to cover individual variation (Morton et al., Br J Sports Med, 2018). Going higher is not harmful but buys little extra muscle.
  • In a caloric deficit (slow recomp) the right move is the upper end — ~1.8–2.2 g/kg — because higher protein spares lean mass while you lose fat (Helms et al., 2014; Longland et al., 2016).
  • For a masters athlete, this matters more, not less — though less than the scare stories imply. Trained over-35 athletes show protein metabolism "indistinguishable from athletes half their age" (Moore et al., 2021; see science §5), so the total doesn't balloon with age — but hitting it consistently and evenly does the work.

Worked example (so the math is concrete). A ~95 kg athlete: 1.6 × 95 ≈ 150 g/day floor, 2.2 × 95 ≈ 210 g/day ceiling. A simple target band of ~165–200 g/day sits comfortably in the recomp-friendly upper half. Substitute your own bodyweight; the band scales linearly.

docs.title: How to hit it without logging
docs.text:
You do not need to weigh food or track macros. Use the palm method plus a per-meal floor:

  • ~0.3–0.4 g/kg protein per meal, which for a ~95 kg athlete is ~30–40 g — roughly one to two palm-sized portions of a protein food per meal. [moderate] Per-meal dosing of ~0.4 g/kg maximizes the muscle-protein-synthesis response (Schoenfeld & Aragon, 2018).
  • Anchor every meal and snack with a protein first, then build carbs/veg/fat around it by eye. If protein leads, the "eyeball the rest" approach lands in a good place by default.
  • 3–5 protein hits a day (meals + a snack or two) spreads the dose. Even distribution beats skewing it all to dinner. [moderate]
  • Rule of thumb to "see" 30–40 g: a chicken breast, a can of tuna, 3 large eggs plus a bit more, a ~150 g tub of Greek yogurt + a scoop of whey, 200 g lean mince, or one scoop of whey (~25 g) topped up with milk or yogurt.

A protein-first day on autopilot (no weighing):

Breakfast   eggs + Greek yogurt, or yogurt + whey + berries        ~35–45 g
Lunch       palm-or-two of chicken/beef/fish/tofu + carbs + veg    ~40–50 g
Snack       whey shake, or cottage cheese, or jerky + milk         ~25–35 g
Dinner      palm-or-two of a protein + carbs + veg                 ~45–55 g
(optional)  casein-ish before bed: yogurt / cottage cheese / milk  ~20–30 g
                                                          total ≈ 165–210 g

What this means for the program. Protein is the only thing worth counting, and even that you can count on your fingers. Lead every meal with a protein, aim for the upper half of the range while recomping, and you've handled ~80% of the nutrition outcome.

docs.title: 2. Total energy: steering a slow recomp without the scale
docs.text:
The goal is slow body recomposition — lose fat, build the chest/back/delt/core physique — judged by the mirror and performance, not bodyweight. That changes how we steer energy.

Why ignore the scale here. [strong, in context] When you simultaneously add muscle and lose fat, the scale can sit flat, drift up, or drift down — all while you visibly improve. Recomposition is well-documented in trained-but-detrained athletes returning to training, exactly this athlete's situation (re-onboarding after a gap). The scale measures the sum of two changes moving in opposite directions, so it's the wrong instrument. We track the mirror, the tape (waist), and the training-log instead.

The energy setting for slow recomp: a small deficit or maintenance, protein held high. [moderate]

  • A mild deficit (~10–20% below maintenance, roughly 200–500 kcal) with high protein and hard training is the best-evidenced recomp setup — fat goes down, lean mass holds or climbs (Longland 2016; Barakat et al. recomposition review, 2020). Aggressive deficits cost muscle and wreck training quality; this is a gentle lever, in keeping with the program's longevity-over-speed ethos.
  • You don't have to count calories to run this. Protein-first eating is intrinsically satiating and tends to auto-regulate intake downward. Steer by outcomes, not arithmetic (below).

docs.title: The no-logging recomp dashboard
docs.text:
Check these on a slow cadence; adjust food by a notch, not a spreadsheet:

Signal Cadence If trending wrong Adjustment Mirror (same light, fasted AM) every ~2–4 wks softer, not leaner trim a notch of carbs/fat at the meals furthest from training Waist / belt notch every ~2–4 wks waist up small deficit nudge; protein stays fixed Training performance / energy (the log) continuous strength or MetCon output sliding, sessions feel flat you've cut too hard — add food back; performance wins Recovery & sleep (readiness trend) continuous readiness dropping, hungry/cold/cranky ease the deficit; recomp is a marathon

The tie-breaker rule: if the mirror and the training log ever disagree, the training log wins. A leaner reflection is not worth a sliding deadlift or a flat Fran. Eat to train; lean comes as a by-product of consistency, not starvation.

What this means for the program. Set a gentle energy deficit (or just maintenance with high protein) and judge it by mirror + waist + performance on a 2–4 week cadence — never by daily bodyweight. Carbs and fats are the adjustment dial; protein is fixed. When in doubt, eat slightly more and let training quality lead. This is recomposition by patience, matching the tendons-first / no-rush philosophy in training-system §1.

docs.title: 3. Fueling around morning training
docs.text: The program trains in the morning (see science §1). Three fueling questions come up: caffeine, the tendon pre-load, and whether to eat before training.

docs.title: 3.1 The espresso (caffeine timing)
docs.text:
Caffeine is the program's strategic morning lever — it counteracts the early-day power dip (science §1). The fueling rules:

  • Dose & timing: ~3 mg/kg (~285 mg for a ~95 kg athlete) taken ~45–60 min pre-session. [strong] That's roughly 2–3 espressos depending on the machine. The performance window peaks ~45–60 min after intake.
  • Reserve it for the hardest sessions — heavy strict strength, skill days, sprinty conditioning — and cycle it rather than dosing every day. Habituation blunts the effect; cycling keeps it potent.
  • AM-only, and titrate down if sleep suffers. [strong] This athlete's main recovery limiter is sleep quality, not duration (decisions), and caffeine has a ~5–6 h half-life — an afternoon dose still has a quarter of it onboard near midnight. Keep it before ~noon; if sleep gets restless, cut the dose before you cut anything else. The full traffic-light gating lives in training-system §4 (caffeine timing).
  • A small caffeine dose is fine on a near-empty stomach for most people; if it bothers your gut, take it with the pre-session collagen + a few carbs (below).

docs.title: 3.2 The tendon pre-load: collagen + vitamin C
docs.text:
This is a small, cheap, on-target intervention for the program's central injury risk — connective tissue (science §5).

  • 15 g hydrolyzed collagen (or gelatin) + ~50 mg vitamin C, taken ~60 min before tendon-loading work. [moderate] Taken an hour pre-load, this roughly doubled a collagen-synthesis marker (Shaw/Baar et al., 2017), and a 2024 RCT found hydrolyzed collagen + resistance training improved patellar-tendon adaptation in middle-aged men. The ~1 h timing matters: the amino acids need to be circulating when the tissue is loaded.
  • Vitamin C is the cofactor for collagen cross-linking — a glass of orange juice (~50 mg) covers it, or a tablet. No need to overdo it.
  • When: the gymnastics / strict-strength / skill days — exactly the sessions adding new upper-body pressing, pulling, dip and false-grip load, where tendon outpaces are the risk. On pure conditioning or wellness days it's optional.
  • Honest evidence note: the collagen-synthesis-marker data are robust; long-term injury-prevention RCTs are still thin. Treat this as low-risk and biologically well-supported, not proven to prevent injury. It's cheap insurance on the program's known weak link, not a magic shield.

Practical stack: the pre-session collagen + vitamin C can ride along with the morning espresso ~45–60 min out — one small ritual covers both the caffeine window and the tendon window.

docs.title: 3.3 Eat before, or train fasted?
docs.text:
• For a ~45–60 min session, fasted is fine if you prefer it — performance over short, mostly strength/skill sessions isn't meaningfully impaired by an overnight fast for most people. [moderate]
  • If a session is long, sprinty, or feels flat fasted, a small carb + protein hit ~30–60 min before helps (e.g. a banana + the whey/collagen, or toast + eggs). Carbs matter more the more glycolytic/MetCon-heavy the day. [moderate]
  • The pre-session collagen counts as a tiny feed — a few grams of protein on board pre-training is mildly anabolic and settles the stomach with caffeine.
  • Don't force a big breakfast before heavy AM work if it sits badly. The priority is the post-session feed (next section); the pre-feed is optional and individual.

What this means for the program. On hard GREEN morning days: ~2–3 espressos (~3 mg/kg) and 15 g collagen + vitamin C, ~45–60 min before training; optional light carb+protein if you train better fed. AM-only caffeine, cycled, dialed down before it touches sleep. On easy/wellness/RED days, skip the caffeine and the pre-load — they're tools for the sessions that need them.

docs.title: 4. Post-session recovery nutrition
docs.text:
Recovery nutrition matters more here than for a typical lifter, for two athlete-specific reasons: this athlete gets very sore and sleeps restlessly, so the levers that blunt soreness and support sleep earn their place.

  • Protein within a few hours, not a frantic 30-min "window." [strong] The "anabolic window" is far wider than gym folklore claims — total daily protein and per-meal dosing matter more than racing a shake (Schoenfeld/Aragon). A normal protein-led meal within ~1–2 h of training is plenty. If the next meal is hours off, a whey shake (~25–40 g) post-session is a convenient way to land a dose.
  • Carbs to refill and to aid recovery. [moderate] After glycolytic MetCons or sprint work, carbs replenish glycogen and, paired with protein, support recovery. You don't need to count them — put a real carb (rice, potato, oats, fruit, bread) on the post-training plate. Bias carbs toward the training meals; that's also the natural place to trim them on lower-output days (see §2).
  • Soreness (DOMS): manage expectations honestly. [strong] No food erases soreness. But adequate protein + total energy + carbs supports the repair process, and a few specifics have modest support:
  • Tart cherry juice has the best DOMS/recovery evidence of the food options — modest reductions in soreness and markers after damaging work (Bell et al. reviews). [moderate] Optional, situational — e.g. after a deliberately hard or novel session.
  • Omega-3s (oily fish / supplement) may modestly reduce soreness and support recovery over time. [suggestive] (See §5.)
  • Stay fed and hydrated. Under-eating amplifies soreness and slows repair more than any "recovery food" fixes it.
  • The sleep-quality lever is partly a nutrition lever. [moderate] Because restless sleep is the #1 recovery limiter here:
  • Protein before bed (casein-like: Greek yogurt, cottage cheese, milk, or a casein shake, ~20–40 g) supplies overnight amino acids and doesn't hurt sleep. [moderate]
  • Keep the evening meal from being too late, too large, or too boozy — all degrade sleep quality.
  • Caffeine discipline (§3.1) is the biggest dietary sleep lever — re-emphasized because it's that important here.
  • Pair this with the program's evening paced-breathing block, the primary non-food sleep tool (science §2).

What this means for the program. After training, eat a normal protein-led meal with real carbs within a couple of hours (a whey shake bridges the gap if the meal is far off). Don't chase a magic window or a magic soreness food — keep total protein and energy adequate, hydrate, and let sleep do the heavy lifting. A pre-bed casein-type protein and strict caffeine timing quietly support the restless-sleep limiter.

docs.title: 5. The weekend-alcohol → Monday-recovery note
docs.text:
Alcohol is social, mostly weekends here, and that's a legitimate choice — this program optimizes for a sustainable, enjoyable life, not monastic compliance. The job of this section is honesty about the cost and a practical way to spend around it.

What the evidence says, plainly. [moderate–strong]

  • Alcohol degrades sleep architecture — it can shorten sleep latency but suppresses REM and fragments the back half of the night. For someone whose limiter is sleep quality, this is the main cost. A boozy Saturday night shows up as a flat, low-readiness Sunday/Monday.
  • Alcohol blunts muscle protein synthesis acutely and can impair recovery and next-day performance, especially with larger doses post-training (Parr et al., 2014). [moderate]
  • It dehydrates and adds "empty" energy — relevant to a recomp, though the bigger lever is sleep and recovery, not the calories per se.

This is not "don't drink." It's "spend it where it costs least, and let the system absorb it."

  • Don't pair heavy drinking with the day's hardest training — especially not right after a muscle-damaging session.
  • Hydrate and get a protein-led meal in around drinking; it won't undo the sleep hit but helps the rest.
  • Expect Monday to read AMBER, and that's fine — the readiness engine is designed for this. A low-readiness Monday after a social weekend should pull the day toward skill/strength + mobility/wellness, not a crushing MetCon. The system gates this automatically (training-system §5 / science §3).
  • Structurally, this is why the week undulates. Putting a hard MetCon on Monday is the wrong default for a weekend-social athlete; the planner biases Monday toward lower-CNS, recovery-friendly work and lets the harder days land mid-week when readiness has rebounded.

What this means for the program. Weekend social drinking stays — it's part of a sustainable life. The program plans around it: don't stack booze on the hardest sessions, hydrate and eat protein, and let Monday autoregulate down. The cost is mostly sleep quality, so the same evening-breathing and caffeine-timing levers that protect sleep are the ones that buffer the weekend.

docs.title: 6. Supplement protocol
docs.text:
A short, evidence-tiered stack — the few things worth taking, and honestly what each does. The athlete currently takes none and is open to suggestions; this is the recommended starting set, ranked by how well it's supported. Nothing here is required to make the program work; supplements are the last ~5%, after protein, energy, sleep, and training consistency.

Supplement Dose What it's for Evidence Creatine monohydrate 5 g/day, every day, any time Strength, power, lean mass, work capacity; some cognitive/recovery upside. Cheap, deeply studied. [strong] — one of the most robustly supported ergogenic aids in existence. No loading phase needed; 5 g/day saturates in ~3–4 weeks. Whey protein 1 scoop (~25 g) as needed to hit the daily target A fast, convenient way to land the protein number — not magic, just efficient protein. [strong] — for total protein adequacy. Whole-food protein is equivalent; whey just makes the target easier on busy days. Collagen + vitamin C 15 g collagen + ~50 mg C, ~60 min pre tendon work Tendon/connective-tissue support — the program's central injury lever (§3.2). [moderate] — strong on collagen-synthesis markers; long-term injury-prevention RCTs still thin. Low-risk, on-target. Vitamin D₃ ~1000–2000 IU/day (more if blood-tested low) Bone, muscle function, immune, mood/sleep support — corrects a deficiency that's common, especially in winter / less sun. [moderate] — clear benefit if you're low (worth a blood test), little extra if you're already replete. Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) ~1–2 g combined EPA+DHA/day (or oily fish 2–3×/wk) Recovery, soreness, anti-inflammatory support; may help sleep. [suggestive–moderate] — real but modest effects; food sources count.

Notes & honesty:

  • Creatine and whey are the two that earn their place unconditionally. If the stack got cut to two, it's these.
  • Creatine causes water retention inside the muscle — a good sign (cell volumization), but it can nudge the scale up a pound or two in the first weeks. Since we ignore the scale anyway (§2), this is a non-issue here — just don't let it spook you.
  • Whey isn't special beyond convenience — it's protein. Skip it if you reliably hit the target with food. It exists in this stack because the athlete is short on time and mixes cooking with convenience.
  • Vitamin D is a "test, don't guess" item — dose to your actual blood level rather than guessing.
  • Skip the rest. BCAAs (redundant if total protein is adequate), fat-burners, most pre-workouts (caffeine already covered), testosterone boosters — not worth it for this program's goals. Caffeine is covered by the espresso (§3.1), so a separate pre-workout is redundant.
  • This is general guidance, not medical advice. Anyone on medication or with a health condition should run a stack past their doctor; the vitamin D blood test is a genuinely worthwhile check.

What this means for the program. Daily creatine 5 g + whey to fill the protein gap are the core. Collagen + vitamin C pre-loads tendon days (§3.2). Vitamin D (dose to bloodwork) and omega-3 round out recovery/sleep support. Everything else is noise for these goals.

docs.title: 7. Fast, high-protein options (for someone short on time)
docs.text:
The athlete mixes cooking and convenience and is often time-pressed, so the default toolkit biases to fast. Each lands ~25–45 g protein with minimal effort. Mix, match, repeat — variety is nice but hitting the number is the point.

No-cook / grab-and-go (~25–40 g): - Greek yogurt (large tub) + whey scoop + berries + honey - Cottage cheese + fruit, or + tomatoes & black pepper (savory) - Whey shake in milk (~35 g) — the fastest 30 s of protein there is - Two cans of tuna or a tin of sardines + crackers / on toast - Beef/biltong jerky + a glass of milk or a shake - Pre-cooked chicken/deli meat roll-ups + cheese - Protein bar (check it's actually ~20 g+ protein, not a candy bar) + milk - A handful of edamame + a yogurt

5–10 minute cook (~30–50 g): - 3–4 egg scramble/omelet + cheese (+ leftover veg/meat thrown in) - Microwave-rice pouch + pre-cooked chicken/prawns + soy/sriracha - Tinned beans or lentils + tinned tuna/eggs on toast - Protein oats: oats + milk + whey + peanut butter (overnight or microwave) - High-protein pasta (lentil/chickpea) + a quick sauce + parmesan - Smoked salmon + eggs + bagel

Batch-once, eat-fast (cook on a quieter day, reheat all week): - A tray of chicken thighs / lean mince / pulled pork → into rice bowls, wraps, salads all week - Hard-boil a half-dozen eggs → instant protein snacks - A big pot of chili or a curry with beans + meat → reheats in minutes, scales protein easily

Eating-out / takeaway defaults (convenience without losing the plot): - Lead with the protein: grilled chicken/steak/fish, burrito bowls (double meat), poke, kebab meat + salad, sushi with extra edamame, burger (even bun-and-all) over fries-as-the-meal. - "Protein first, eyeball the rest" works at a restaurant too — pick the dish around its protein and the rest sorts itself.

What this means for the program. Keep 3–4 of these on permanent autopilot so a busy day still hits ~165–200 g without thought. Convenience is not the enemy of the goal — missing the protein number is. A whey shake and a yogurt are a perfectly legitimate "meal" on a wrecked day.

docs.title: 8. The whole thing, in one paragraph
docs.text: Eat protein first and hit ~1.6–2.2 g/kg/day (~165–200 g for a ~95 kg athlete) by leading every meal with a palm-or-two of a protein food — that one habit is ~80% of the result, and you can count it on your fingers. Eyeball the rest, steering a gentle slow-recomp by the mirror, the waist, and the training log — never the scale (carbs/fats are the dial; if performance ever slips, eat more). On hard morning days, take ~3 mg/kg caffeine (a couple of espressos) and 15 g collagen + vitamin C ~45–60 min pre, keep caffeine AM-only and cycled so it never touches your restless sleep, then recover with a protein-led meal + real carbs and a pre-bed casein-type protein. Weekend drinking stays — just don't stack it on the hardest session, and let Monday autoregulate down. Take creatine 5 g daily and whey to fill gaps (the two that always earn their place), plus collagen+C, vitamin D, and omega-3 for tendons and recovery. Everything else is noise. This is fueling for longevity and consistency, matching the rest of the program — non-dogmatic, sustainable, and built to be forked.

docs.title: Science Foundations
docs.text:
This document explains the evidence basis for the key design choices in this training program. It is written so that anyone who forks this repo can understand why the program is built the way it is, and can judge for themselves how strongly each choice is supported.

A few ground rules for reading this:

  • Honesty about uncertainty is a feature. Where the evidence is strong, it says so. Where a recommendation rests on coaching consensus, mechanism, or extrapolation from adjacent research rather than a direct trial, it says that too.
  • Most of the underlying studies were done on younger, often untrained, often male lab subjects. This program is built for a 39-year-old, long-trained athlete re-onboarding after a detraining gap. Directions of effect transfer well; exact magnitudes may be smaller and more variable for an experienced athlete near his structural ceiling.
  • The program targets wellness, longevity, confidence, and physique — not competition or PRs. Recommendations are tuned for consistency and tissue health over squeezing out maximal performance.
  • Each section ends with a "What this means for the program." summary.

The athlete profile used throughout (so the reasoning is concrete) is a fast-twitch, lower-body-dominant athlete who trains in the morning, wants upper-body gymnastics strength (strict handstand push-ups and muscle-ups), a bigger chest and visible core, better hip/hamstring mobility — explicitly not more leg size — and wants long-distance capacity (a 10-mile road race available year-round) without chasing speed. The athlete also carries an asymmetric structural quirk (a noticeable left/right imbalance) and a restless-but-adequate sleep pattern, both of which shape specific choices below. Forkers should substitute their own profile; the principles are general.

docs.title: 1. Morning Training
docs.text:
The program runs in the morning, by choice and logistics. This raises a fair worry: isn't the body weaker and less powerful early in the day?

The acute deficit is real, and it is largest for explosive power. Maximal strength tends to peak in the late afternoon/early evening (~16:00–20:00) and is lowest in the early morning (~06:00–10:00). The swing is biggest for explosive, anaerobic tasks: across roughly 22 Wingate studies, peak power was ~2–10% better and mean power ~2–12% better in the afternoon/evening (Souissi et al., 2004/2007). Slow maximal strength swings much less — a 2024 team-sport meta-analysis found countermovement jump and agility significantly worse in the morning but no significant time-of-day effect on isometric strength or squat jump (Frontiers Sports & Active Living, 2024; PMC11782285). A fast-twitch, power-dominant athlete will tend to feel this morning dip more on explosive work than a slow-strength athlete would.

The cause is mostly peripheral — and that's why it's fixable. Reviews attribute the morning-to-evening rise primarily to lower core/muscle temperature and intrinsic (non-neural) muscle factors, not to reduced neural drive (APS/Physiology, 2021; PMC8425416). Caffeine raises morning strength without changing muscle electrical activity, which is consistent with a peripheral, not central, limiter. Because the problem is largely "the muscle is cold," it responds to interventions that warm it up.

Three levers shrink or erase the morning deficit:

  1. An extended, active warm-up. Raising morning body temperature to match the afternoon largely abolishes the explosive-power difference. A 15-minute active warm-up produced higher morning Wingate power than a 5-minute one, and a prolonged warm-up improved morning loaded jumps (Racinais/Souissi, Int J Sports Med, 2011; PMID 21305444). Active beats passive warm-up for jump height.
  2. Caffeine (~3 mg/kg, ~45–60 min pre-session). A triple-blind RCT showed caffeine raised back-squat velocity and power in both morning and evening and counteracted the morning decline at light loads, again without altering muscle electrical activity (Eur J Appl Physiol, 2025; PMC12528362). A 2025 female crossover restored morning neuromuscular performance to evening levels. This is best reserved for the hardest morning sessions (heavy strict strength, skill work, sprinty conditioning); halving or skipping it on low-recovery days protects sleep and caffeine sensitivity.
  3. Habit (diurnal specificity). Training at a fixed hour raises performance specifically at that hour. After weeks of morning-only training, the usual morning-to-evening strength gap disappeared in morning-trained groups but persisted in evening-trained controls (Sedliak; Grgic et al., 2019 meta-analysis, Chronobiology International; PMID 30704301). Mechanistically, early training can shift the skeletal-muscle circadian clock earlier (shown in mice).

Crucially, time of day does not change long-term gains. The Grgic et al. (2019) meta-analysis concluded that absolute strength and hypertrophy gains do not differ between morning and evening training. Morning lifting builds just as much muscle and strength; the only durable difference is when that strength is best expressed. So a morning lift may read slightly below a lifetime best set at another time — that is an expression effect, not lost capacity.

One sequencing note: avoid long static stretching right before explosive morning work, which can blunt power; use dynamic, ramped movement instead (PMC3734300). More on this in §3 and §6.

Caveat: most of these data come from young men in lab settings; for an experienced 39-year-old, magnitudes are likely smaller and more variable. Chronotype matters — a true evening-type forced into mornings may retain a residual deficit longer.

What this means for the program. Morning training does not cap strength or hypertrophy — judge progress by trend over weeks, not by chasing an evening-style 1RM. Build a real ramp-up (target ~12–15 minutes of active, temperature-raising work, finishing with progressive ramp sets) before any heavy or explosive piece; this is where the morning power deficit gets erased. Use caffeine strategically and only on the hardest sessions: a full ~3 mg/kg dose taken in the morning (~45–60 min pre), reserved for heavy strict-strength, skill, or sprinty days, and cycled rather than taken daily — since this athlete's main recovery limiter is sleep quality, not duration, AM-only timing and a willingness to titrate the dose down if sleep suffers protect the larger recovery lever. Place fresh-CNS skill and strength work (strict HSPU, muscle-up strength, heavy pressing) early in the primed window, conditioning after. Expect morning output to climb in the first 4–8 weeks partly from clock adaptation — don't mistake it for fleeting "newbie magic."

docs.title: 2. Meditation and Stretching Integration
docs.text:
The program treats a ~20-minute mindfulness + mobility block as first-class programming, not an optional add-on. Here is what that block can and cannot do.

Mindfulness reliably nudges the stress and recovery systems — modestly. A meta-analysis of mindfulness interventions found a small-to-moderate reduction in salivary cortisol (Hedges g ≈ 0.41, p = 0.025), with moderate heterogeneity (Sanada et al., Frontiers in Physiology, 2016). A broader 2023 meta-analysis found only small effects on inflammation and stress biomarkers (PMC10003032). The effect is real but should not be oversold.

It raises vagally-mediated HRV and parasympathetic tone — directly relevant to a program driven by a wearable readiness score. A 10-day online RCT showed increased HRV during both day and night in the mindfulness group, alongside sleep-quality gains (PMC7746169).

The single most reliable lever inside a 20-minute block is slow-paced breathing, not meditation content per se. Breathing near ~6 breaths/min (~0.1 Hz, an individual "resonance frequency") maximizes respiratory sinus arrhythmia and raises time-domain HRV (SDNN, RMSSD) in a dose-response way (Laborde et al., PMC8656666; Steffen et al., PMC5575449). Resonance frequency is individual and best found empirically with a wearable (test ~4.5–6.5 breaths/min). This argues for scripting a meaningful chunk of the block as explicit paced breathing rather than open "just sit" time.

In athletes specifically, the wellness case is strong. Mindfulness practice is associated with better sleep, lower pre-sleep arousal and anxiety, improved mood and "readiness to train," and markedly lower next-day injury risk — NCAA Division I athletes were roughly "half as likely" to be injured the day after practicing (McGehee et al., PMC10916770; rowers' sleep/performance data, Front. Psychol., 2020). The injury figure is observational (mindful athletes may already be lower-risk), so treat it as an association, not proven causation.

Now the honest part about stretching. Two findings reshape how mobility is programmed:

  • Stretching does not meaningfully reduce soreness/DOMS. A Cochrane review (12 studies, including a 2377-person trial) found no effect of pre/post stretching on DOMS at 24/48/72 h versus passive recovery (Herbert et al., CD004577). Its value is range-of-motion and movement quality, not recovery. Frame it honestly: "moves better / feels better," not "less sore."
  • Long static holds before training transiently impair strength and power, but only when misused. A 104-study meta-analysis found acute static stretching reduced strength −5.4%, power −1.9%, explosive −2.0%; the effect is dose-dependent — ≤60 s per muscle is a trivial 1–2% impairment, while >60 s costs 4.0–7.5% (Simic et al., Scand J Med Sci Sports, 2013). Complex tasks like jumps and sprints were not significantly impaired. Short holds inside a full warm-up are harmless and may even lower injury risk.

The implication is about placement: keep long static and PNF holds out of the immediate pre-lift window, and keep the seated meditation block separate from the sympathetic spike of training (post-session or evening), where it best supports next-day HRV and sleep. Foam rolling is the better pre-session mobility tool — it raises ROM without the strength penalty (Wiewelhove et al., Sports Medicine, 2022).

What this means for the program. Treat the 20-minute block as non-negotiable programming, justified mainly by sleep, stress, mood, injury-risk reduction, and parasympathetic tone — not by a big performance jump (effects are modestly sized). Build explicit ~6-breath/min paced breathing into 5–10 minutes of it. Anchor the full block post-workout or in the evening; a 3–5 minute breathing reset right after training is a fine bonus, but the main dose belongs away from the training spike. For an athlete whose sleep is adequate in hours but restless in quality, the evening paced-breathing dose is doing double duty — it is the program's primary lever on the single most important recovery input (sleep quality), so treat it as a sleep tool, not just a stress tool. Keep pre-WOD mobility to foam rolling, dynamic work, and short (≤30–60 s) targeted holds. Stop selling stretching as soreness relief; sell it as ROM and movement quality, with sleep and load management doing the real recovery work.

docs.title: 3. Readiness Autoregulation
docs.text:
The program adapts the daily session to a readiness signal (wearable HRV/recovery score plus a subjective check-in). The user can say "I'm exhausted, busy day" and the system de-loads. Here is why that is sound — and where its limits are.

Autoregulation matches or beats fixed plans while requiring less hard work. A meta-analysis of 8 RCTs found HRV-guided endurance prescription produced equal or greater aerobic improvement than predefined training, often in less time, because hard sessions land on days the athlete can absorb them (MDPI Applied Sciences, 2020, 10:8532). An 11-week HRV-guided HIFT/CrossFit-style RCT showed the guided group did fewer moderate/high-intensity sessions yet reached similar-to-better fitness (MDPI JFMK, 2021, 6:102). The benefit is most visible in reducing non-responders and negative responders and protecting vagal recovery — i.e., it is a risk-management and consistency tool, not a magic performance multiplier (Manresa-Rocamora et al., PMC8507742).

It helps strength/hypertrophy too, not just endurance. A 7-week resistance-training study in older women compared HRV-individualized recovery (train when RMSSD returned toward baseline) against a fixed schedule; the individualized group showed greater and notably more consistent gains in 1RM, muscle CSA, and rate of force development (Front. Physiol., 2024; PMC11685108). A systematic review confirms both subjective (RPE/RIR) and objective (HRV, velocity-loss) autoregulation can enhance strength versus fixed loading (PMC7810043).

Subjective check-ins are as good as or better than the wearable. A systematic review concluded that "subjective self-reported measures trump commonly used objective measures" for tracking training response, including HRV (Saw, Main & Gastin, Br J Sports Med, 2016; PMC4789708). The validated minimal instrument is a short morning score (e.g., sleep, fatigue, stress, soreness). Best practice is mixed-methods: the subjective score and the HRV trend — so a self-reported "I'm wrecked" should be able to force a back-off regardless of what the number says.

The validated engine is a trend, not a single number. The standard operational rule computes morning lnRMSSD, takes a 7-day rolling average, and compares it against a personal Smallest Worthwhile Change (SWC) band, typically baseline mean ± 0.5 × SD from a 1–2 week familiarization (Vesterinen/Nuuttila protocols). A rising coefficient of variation (instability), not just a low single value, flags accumulating fatigue. This maps cleanly onto traffic-light logic:

  • GREEN — rolling average within/above the SWC band, stable variability: run the planned hard/heavy/sprinty work, with fresh-CNS skill work placed early.
  • AMBER — at the lower edge of the band, or rising variability/moderate fatigue: keep low-fatigue priorities (skill/strength practice) but cut conditioning volume ~30–50% and drop intensity (Zone 2 instead of sprints; lighter loads).
  • RED — below the band (or a multi-day downward trend, or the manual override): the default offer is the 20-minute breathing/meditation + mobility block plus an easy flush — de-loaded, not crushed. The wellness block is an offer, not a mandate: on a genuinely bad day a true full rest day is a legitimate choice, and forcing token activity over real rest is the wrong trade. The principle is "don't dig the hole," not "always do something."

What to scale, in order: intensity and high-CNS-demand work first, then volume, then modality — on most red days keep something moving, but allow a true rest day when the signal (and the athlete) says so.

Wearable caveats matter. Consumer wearables are accurate enough for HRV/RHR trends (e.g., HRV concordance CCC ≈ 0.94, MAPE ≈ 8% vs ECG; Bellenger et al., Sensors, 2021), but proprietary "recovery %" scores are opaque and silently updated — treat them as one directional input, not gospel. Interpretation has traps: in fitter athletes a chronically high HRV can signal parasympathetic saturation (coasting), and a low value can mean either sympathetic overactivity or vagal saturation — so direction and context beat the absolute number. Masters athletes have a lower parasympathetic baseline than younger athletes, so the SWC must be built from the athlete's own baseline, not population norms, and re-baselined every 4–6 weeks during re-onboarding.

Caveats: most RCT evidence is in endurance populations; direct trials in functional-fitness or in trained 39-year-old men are sparse, and the strongest resistance-training data is in older women (n = 21). HRV reliably detects overreaching, but few studies are powered to show that HRV-guided plans reduce injuries specifically — so the injury-prevention rationale is plausible and indirect, not firmly established.

What this means for the program. Build the readiness band from the athlete's own data (14-day familiarization), not the wearable's generic color. Gate each day on a hybrid signal, and let the subjective override always win. Use the GREEN/AMBER/RED logic above, scaling intensity before volume before modality. Watch the trend (multi-day direction and variability), not a single reading, and remember a sudden very-high HRV after hard weeks can mean overreaching, not recovery. Sell autoregulation as what the evidence supports: consistency, fewer bad responses, and not digging a hole on a bad day — exactly the "science-based but not over-optimized" fit.

docs.title: 4. Skill Progressions (Strict HSPU and Strict Muscle-Up)
docs.text: Both target skills are strength projects built from progressions, not feats of skill luck. No HSPU- or muscle-up-specific RCT exists, so the pathways are well-triangulated coaching consensus; the supporting mechanisms (eccentric overload, training frequency) are peer-reviewed but extrapolated from adjacent strength and tendon research. Treat prerequisite numbers as heuristics to be measured against, not validated cutoffs.

docs.title: Strict Handstand Push-Up
docs.text:
There is a real strength prerequisite. Coaching and strength-standard sources converge: the ability to press roughly bodyweight overhead is the practical ceiling, with a ~0.65–0.75× bodyweight strict press as the "intermediate gateway." Because the HSPU loads ~60–70%+ of bodyweight onto the shoulders, a strict press near bodyweight provides the needed margin — which is exactly why the lift is genuinely hard for tall, heavy athletes (long levers raise difficulty), and why deliberately adding top-heavy upper-body mass improves the strength-to-leverage ratio.

The consensus pathway is a graded vertical-pressing progression: full-ROM push-up → dip → box pike push-up → wall pike → deficit pike → wall HSPU negatives → strict HSPU. Each step raises the percentage of bodyweight pressed and verticalizes the torso toward the HSPU pattern (Barbell Physio; Training Think Tank; CrossFit.com). Seated/strict dumbbell press is the off-wall builder for athletes not yet comfortable inverting.

Eccentric (negative) overload is the highest-yield breakthrough tool. Slow (3–5 s) wall HSPU negatives let the athlete train the full ROM at a load above concentric capacity. Meta-analyses show eccentric-only training raises eccentric strength ~27% (vs ~10% for concentric-only), with gains specific to the trained mode, and the most reliable results come from protocols of ≥8 weeks (Springer Sports Medicine-Open, 2025).

HSPU is more than pressing strength. It also demands shoulder-flexion ROM, inverted scapular control, and midline stability; trunk, triceps, and handstand-hold strength each independently cap capacity. So wall holds, hollow-body work, and overhead mobility belong in the progression, not just pressing reps.

docs.title: Strict Ring Muscle-Up
docs.text:
Clear strict gates come before transition work, not after. Converging coaching consensus: roughly 8–10 strict dead-hang pull-ups, 3–5 chest-to-sternum (high) pull-ups, and 5–10 strict ring dips from a full lockout, plus a solid false-grip hang and false-grip ring rows to the lower chest (WODprep; Barbell Physio; HWPO). The single best predictor of transition success is pull height — how high you can pull the rings down your torso.

The false grip is the most common true bottleneck — more than raw pulling power. It mechanically shortens the arm, keeping the rings close so the elbows can rotate over the top; a neutral grip makes a strict transition essentially impossible regardless of strength (Rogue; Forge the Flow).

Eccentrics again do double duty. Slow muscle-up negatives and false-grip eccentric work build the specific transition strength and are the primary tendon-health stimulus. A 4-week eccentric program shifted EMG signatures during static ring elements in gymnasts (PMC8956077), and broader tendon literature shows high-load eccentric work drives the greatest collagen and stiffness adaptation (PMC7739434).

The assistance ladder, easiest to hardest: false-grip hangs and ring rows → banded / foot-assisted transitions → jumping muscle-ups into a slow eccentric → low-ring feet-on- ground strict reps → full strict rep (Bulldog Gear; WODprep; ABrotherAbroad). Each rung trains the high-pull-into-transition that is the actual sticking point.

Wrist and elbow are the at-risk tissues. In the false grip, load is borne by the flexed wrist and elbow; rushing false-grip volume is the dominant cause of medial/anterior elbow and wrist pain. Build false-grip hang time gradually (e.g., 30–45 s of total hang before high-volume false-grip pulling), and treat early elbow/wrist pain as a signal to reduce volume, not push through.

docs.title: Shared principles
docs.text:
Frequency over per-session intensity. When weekly volume is equated, frequency has little independent effect — its value is letting you accumulate quality volume without fatigue spikes (Grgic et al., 2018; Schoenfeld/Grgic frequency meta-analyses). Practically, 2–3 submaximal, never-to-failure exposures per week ("greasing the groove," ~40–60% of max reps) build the pattern and strength while protecting re-onboarding tissue. The two skills reinforce each other: pressing, pulling, scapular control, and midline work transfer across both, and toward the chest/back hypertrophy goals.

What this means for the program. Measure baselines first (strict press 1RM; max strict pull-ups and ring dips; false-grip hang and rows), then program against the gates. Run the HSPU pathway in phases (box/wall pike and shoulder control → deficit pike and midline → wall HSPU negatives as the breakthrough), re-testing around weeks 8–16. Build the muscle-up from its strict gates with eccentrics as the engine and false-grip tissue prep front-loaded. Use 2–3 submaximal exposures/week, place skill work fresh and early, advance only when ~5 controlled clean reps are owned, and trim eccentric volume first (not conditioning) on red days, since eccentric work is the most tendon- and damage-stressful. Expect a multi-month horizon, judged on progression milestones, not calendar dates.

docs.title: 5. Masters-Athlete Adjustments
docs.text:
A 39-year-old, 12-year-trained athlete is not a 25-year-old — but the differences are narrower and more specific than "everything is harder now." The single most important adjustment is about connective tissue.

Muscle and hypertrophy potential are essentially intact. The large "anabolic resistance" of aging is mostly a feature of untrained older adults; in trained masters (>35 yr), protein metabolism is "indistinguishable from athletes half their age" (Moore et al., Sports Medicine, 2021; PMC8566396). Meaningful fiber-size attenuation appears mainly in 60+ cohorts, not 35–45. Practical dosing: ~0.3–0.4 g/kg protein per meal (~30–40 g for a ~95 kg athlete), ~0.5 g/kg post-training, ~1.6–2.2 g/kg/day total. For top-heavy upper-body growth, the constraint is training volume + protein + time, not age.

Recovery from muscle-damaging work is meaningfully slower — but stimulus-dependent. Masters athletes take longer to recover function after high-intensity/eccentric/impact work (e.g., downhill running) than training-matched younger athletes, even with similar damage markers (Doering et al., J Sci Med Sport, 2019). Notably, a strength-matched eccentric squat study found no masters disadvantage — so the slower-recovery caveat applies most to high-rep impact and fatiguing MetCons, less to controlled strength sets (PMC8461049).

Tendon/connective tissue is the governor. This is the biggest genuine 39-vs-25 difference. Collagen turnover and stiffness response begin declining in the mid-30s, non-enzymatic crosslinks accumulate and stiffen tissue, and tendon adapts on a ~12-week clock versus days-to-weeks for muscle — so strength routinely outpaces tendon readiness, the classic overuse-injury setup (J Appl Physiol, 2016). Disuse hits tendon hard and asymmetrically (2 weeks of immobilization cut tendon collagen synthesis ~80% in older men). For an athlete adding new upper-body gymnastics load, this is the central risk.

Heavy-Slow Resistance (HSR) is the best-evidenced way to build tendon capacity — controlled ~3–4 s tempo, heavy loads, ~3×/week for 12+ weeks. HSR improved patellar and Achilles tendinopathy with durable results superior to corticosteroid injection at follow-up, and shifted the collagen crosslink profile favorably (Kongsgaard et al., 2009; Beyer et al., 2015). Mapped to this program: tempo strict dips and ring dips, tempo strict pull-ups, slow eccentric HSPU negatives, and paused dumbbell/incline pressing build both the chest and the tendon stiffness to tolerate muscle-ups and HSPU.

In CrossFit, the shoulder is the dominant injury site (up to ~40% of injuries; rotator- cuff tendons ~45% of those), with the highest-risk movements being exactly the targeted gymnastics skills — kipping pull-ups, ring dips, overhead/Olympic lifts (JSSM CrossFit epidemiology; MDPI JFMK, 2025). This argues strongly for building strict strength and tissue capacity first and limiting high-rep kipping volume — which happens to align with the strict-HSPU / strict-MU goals.

Pre-loading collagen is a cheap, on-target lever. 15 g gelatin or hydrolyzed collagen + ~50 mg vitamin C taken 30–60 min before tendon-loading work roughly doubled a collagen- synthesis marker (Shaw/Baar et al., Am J Clin Nutr, 2017; PMC5183725), and a 2024 RCT found hydrolyzed collagen plus 12 weeks of resistance training enhanced patellar-tendon adaptation in middle-aged men (PMC11917387). Long-term injury-prevention RCTs are still limited, so treat this as low-risk and biologically well-supported rather than proven to prevent injury.

Deloads are autoregulated, not fixed. Light weeks aid long-term progress by managing fatigue; for an athlete re-onboarding from detraining, deload frequency should flex with readiness and life stress (roughly every 4–6 weeks as a default, pulled earlier by red days). Volume dose-response work shows hypertrophy keeps rising with volume but strength shows steep diminishing returns — so the strength goals here do not need high volume, they need consistency and tissue tolerance (Pelland et al., 2025).

Frequency is the safety net on a compressed week. Maximal strength is maintained on as little as 1–2 sessions/week once built (Spiering et al., maintenance review; and the frequency meta-analyses in §4) — it is hypertrophy, driven by accumulated weekly volume, that suffers most when sessions are cut. This matters for a busy-week floor: when life collapses the week to two sessions, the right move is to protect the strict-skill/strength stimulus and one short quality conditioning hit (plus the wellness/mobility block) and let hypertrophy volume be the thing that's temporarily underfed, rather than trying to cram every goal into two days and recovering from none of them.

Caveats: the slower-recovery finding is stimulus-dependent and partly conflicting; precise 39-vs-25 hypertrophy gaps in a 12-year-trained athlete are inferred, not directly measured (likely small); tendon timelines come largely from lower-limb studies and are extrapolated to shoulder/elbow. At 39, individual recovery variability (sleep, work stress, nutrition) likely outweighs chronological age.

What this means for the program. Treat tendons, not muscle or lungs, as the limiter: progress new upper-body pressing/pulling/dip volume slowly (~+10%/week or less) and hold back even when it feels easy, because strength will outpace tissue. Build strict before kipping. Program the new upper-body work HSR-style (heavy, slow tempo, judged over 12+ week blocks). Pre-load collagen + vitamin C ~60 min before gymnastics days. Hit protein hard and evenly. Re-onboard conditioning conservatively — bias early MetCons toward low-impact machines (bike/row/ski) to spare joints while rebuilding the engine — and autoregulate deloads via the readiness score rather than fixing them on a calendar.

docs.title: 6. Mobility Methods (Hip and Hamstring ROM)
docs.text:
The mobility goal is "feel better / move better" — more hip and hamstring range — with no added leg size or strength. The method choices below follow directly from that constraint and from what the evidence says actually drives durable ROM.

Loaded, full-ROM work is the highest-value method here. A systematic review found resistance training through full range produces ROM gains equal to stretching while also building strength through that range, with no moderating effect of age, sex, or training duration (Afonso/Behm et al., Sports Medicine, 2023; PMC9935664). A 2024 RCT confirmed full-ROM resistance training and static stretching produced equal flexibility gains, but resistance training additionally improved hip and back extensor strength (PMC11212372). The catch: gains are specific to the ranges actually loaded, so the movement must reach deep hip flexion / full hamstring length. For someone strong-but-tight, this is ideal — it re-purposes light, deep-range movement as "mobility that happens to load the tissue," without a hypertrophy stimulus (loads stay light).

Most early "tightness" is the nervous system, not short muscle. Early ROM gains are predominantly increased stretch/pain tolerance, not tissue lengthening; under slow passive stretch the limit is subject sensation, and static stretching does not reliably lengthen fascicles or change muscle architecture (PMC12152101; PMC10271914). For a powerful, lower-body-dominant athlete whose nervous system aggressively guards end range, teaching it to produce force where it currently braces — i.e., loading the end range — addresses the actual limiter.

For the hamstrings specifically, long-length eccentrics add a structural adaptation that stretching doesn't. Eccentric training at long muscle length increased biceps femoris fascicle length ~9.3% (vs ~4.9% at short length), letting the muscle work effectively over a greater range while also building eccentric strength and resilience (Front. Physiol., 2016; PMC4971444). Light RDLs / single-leg RDLs with a slow 3–4 s lowering to true end range, plus an occasional Nordic regression, are the best single tool for durable hamstring ROM.

PNF is not durably better than static stretching, so pick the most adherable method. Across hamstring studies, most found no significant difference between PNF and static for chronic ROM (J Sport Rehab, 2018; PMID 28182516). Both beat ballistic/dynamic for end range. The extra complexity of PNF isn't justified for a general-fitness athlete — pick whatever gets done daily.

The effective dose is small. Flexibility gains are largely captured at ~4 minutes per session and ~10 minutes per week per muscle group, with diminishing returns beyond that; individual holds need not exceed ~30 s, and frequency matters more than long single holds (PMC10980866). This makes a short daily floor routine genuinely low-cost.

Timeline and maintenance. Meaningful change appears in ~2–5 weeks and accrues over ~5–15 weeks (average effect size ~0.93). Gains are durable — they persist above baseline for weeks after stopping — but slowly regress, and only "active" individuals retain them, so this is a permanent low-dose habit, not a fixed-end project (detraining meta-analysis, PMC12638565).

The Jefferson curl / deep-range loaded work builds controllable end-range hip and spine ROM under load, with the evidence-based proviso that loads stay light and progress slowly. The loaded-spinal-flexion debate (McGill-line caution vs tissue-adaptation view) is a load-management issue, not a contraindication: used as a light, slow-tempo, progressive tolerance-builder — not a 1RM lift — it is a reasonable middle ground. It also doubles as core/midline work. One athlete-specific qualifier: where there is a structural spinal consideration (e.g. an asymmetric curvature) or any low-back history, the conservative default is to favour controlled, very-light end-range work over aggressive loaded flexion, to start lighter and progress slower than the general guidance, and to clear loaded end-range spinal flexion with a physio screen first. Asymptomatic and unrestricted is not a reason to load fast; it is a reason to load carefully.

Placement protects strength. Long static holds (>60 s/muscle) before lifting transiently cut force, but chronic flexibility work has no negative effect on strength — often neutral-to- slightly-positive (multilevel meta-analysis, PMC11336295; and §2). Any acute deficit recovers in ~10–20 minutes, but the clean fix is to keep long holds out of the pre-lift warm-up and put them at the end of the session or in the separate recovery block.

Caveats: most ROM data come from general/untrained populations, so magnitudes may be smaller and slower for a long-trained athlete near his structural ceiling. How much of any individual's tightness is neural guarding vs genuine architecture is unknown without assessment.

What this means for the program. Make loaded full-ROM lifting the primary mobility driver — light deep-range hip work (cossack squats, deep ATG split squats, elevated-heel deep squats) and long-length hamstring eccentrics (slow light RDLs) — explicitly kept light so it serves range/control, not size. Lean on the unilateral versions of these (single-leg RDLs, split squats, Cossacks) where there is a left/right asymmetry to address: they train each side independently and let the weaker/tighter side catch up rather than letting the dominant side carry the pattern. Add a light Jefferson curl (empty/very light bar, ultra-slow, 2–3×/week) that doubles as midline work — kept extra-conservative, and screened first, where there is a spinal or low-back consideration (see above). Run a short daily static floor routine (90/90, couch stretch, hamstring/pancake holds, ~30 s each) inside the 20-minute recovery block — static is fine, skip PNF complexity; aim for ~10 min/week per area, which captures most of the benefit. Keep long static holds out of the morning warm-up. Expect to feel looser in 2–4 weeks and see measured change by ~6–12 weeks (track toe-touch reach, deep-squat hold, 90/90 photos), then maintain. This work is low-systemic-cost, so it stays in on red days — the ideal de-loaded fallback session.

docs.title: 7. Upper-Body Balance (Hypertrophy and Concurrent Training)
docs.text:
The physique goal is a bigger chest, a stronger/visible core, and a more balanced upper-body — without losing conditioning and without adding leg size. The evidence is encouraging on every count.

Hypertrophy follows a dose-response in weekly hard sets per muscle. More than ~9 sets/week beats lower volumes, with most gains captured around ~10–20 sets and diminishing (but non-zero) returns toward ~20–25 (Schoenfeld et al., 2017; Pelland et al. dose-response meta-regression, 2024–25). To actually bias growth toward chest and back, those muscles must be deliberately counted and pushed into that range while legs are held at maintenance — in the 11–18 set range it takes roughly 8.5 extra weekly sets per additional detectable increment, so volume should be spent where growth is wanted.

The concurrent-training "interference effect" is much weaker than feared. A 43-study meta-analysis found that adding aerobic training did not meaningfully reduce maximal strength or hypertrophy, regardless of age, training status, or endurance modality (Schumann et al., 2022; PMC9474354). The one robust casualty is explosive/power output (~28% reduction with heavy endurance) — a quality this athlete explicitly no longer chases. So building upper-body muscle while keeping MetCons is fully viable.

Where interference does appear, modality and order are the levers. In the classic meta-analysis, concurrent running (not cycling) caused the only significant strength/ hypertrophy decrements (Wilson et al., 2012), and over multi-week programs lifting before endurance preserved lower-body dynamic strength ~6.9% better than endurance-first (Eddens et al., 2017). This favors rowing/bike/ski-erg and short sprinty MetCons over high-volume running, and a lift-first session order on combined days.

A polarized conditioning distribution serves a fast-twitch athlete who also wants long-distance capacity. Polarized training — most aerobic work easy (Zone 2) and a small fraction genuinely hard, with little in the muddy middle — outperforms threshold-heavy distributions for aerobic development in trained athletes (Stöggl & Sperlich, Front. Physiol., 2014; Rosenblat et al. meta-analysis, 2019). It maps almost perfectly onto this athlete's preferences and physiology: keep the high-intensity end as the short, sharp, high-quality sprint and hill-sprint work he is built for and enjoys, and build the "10 miles is always doable" base out of low-stress Zone-2 volume (preferably on low-impact machines early in re-onboarding) rather than a diet of moderate-hard tempo running. Because the goal is capacity, not a race time, this base needs no peak and no taper — it is maintained year-round at a low, sustainable dose, which is exactly what a polarized, mostly-easy base costs.

Hypertrophy is load-flexible and does not require failure. Growth is comparable across ~30–80% 1RM when effort is high, and training to failure gives only a trivial hypertrophy advantage over leaving 1–3 reps in reserve (Refalo/Robinson et al., 2023; PMC9935748). This means chest and back can be grown with gymnastics-friendly tools — rings, dips, weighted pull-ups, higher-rep dumbbell work, slow eccentrics — stopping 1–2 reps shy to manage fatigue and protect MetCon quality and aging shoulders. Failure adds disproportionate neuromuscular fatigue for little extra growth (PMC9908800), which is why RIR-based autoregulation fits.

The physique work and the skill work are the same work. Weighted/strict pull-ups, ring rows, and muscle-up transition negatives build the strict muscle-up and lat/upper-back thickness; pike-to-deficit push-ups, ring dips, and dumbbell pressing build the strict HSPU and chest/shoulders. Programmed as the lifting block and counted as chest/back sets, the gymnastics and hypertrophy goals reinforce rather than compete.

A visible, strong midline is built by training the trunk like a muscle — loaded, near-failure, anti-movement — not endless sit-ups. Anti-extension (ab-wheel/ring fallouts, RKC plank, deficit hollow holds), anti-rotation (Pallof), and loaded carries produce the highest trunk activation and stiffness; abs respond to progressive load like any muscle, while visibility is largely a body-fat function (PMC11036226; PMC11042841).

Caveats: most concurrent-training and dose-response data come from conventional resistance + steady-state endurance, not high-intensity CrossFit MetCons, which combine glycolytic conditioning with eccentric/load stress — so real-world recovery cost may be modestly higher than the null findings suggest, making readiness-driven autoregulation important. Per-muscle set targets are population averages with wide individual variation; the 12–18 range is a starting prescription to tune by recovery and visible progress. Gymnastics timelines are highly individual, and tall stature is a genuine leverage disadvantage for pressing/pulling skills.

What this means for the program. Explicitly count weekly hard sets: target ~12–18 sets/week each for chest and back/pulling (the upper-end growth zone), while capping legs at ~6–9 maintenance sets to hold size/strength with minimal fatigue. Make the gymnastics progressions double as the hypertrophy block. Order sessions lifting-first, MetCon-second on combined days, and favor erg/bike conditioning over high-volume running. Run conditioning polarized — mostly easy Zone-2 base plus a small, high-quality dose of the sprints and hill sprints a fast-twitch athlete is built for — and hold a year-round, un-peaked aerobic base so long-distance capacity stays available without a training block aimed at a date. Train chest and back near-but-not-to-failure (1–3 RIR) across a load spectrum with shoulder-friendly tools. Build the core deliberately with loaded anti-movement work and carries, paired with staying lean enough to see it; bias toward anti-rotation (Pallof, suitcase carries) and single-arm/single-leg work where a left/right imbalance needs correcting, so the asymmetry is trained down rather than reinforced by bilateral loading. Re-onboard conservatively — start chest/back near the low end (~8–10 sets) and ramp ~1–2 sets/muscle/week toward target — and let readiness flex daily volume, which costs almost nothing in long-run growth when training stays sub-failure. The MetCons can stay: they don't meaningfully blunt the physique, and the only real interference cost lands on a quality no longer being chased.

docs.title: A note on the evidence quality
docs.text: Several primary sources (notably some PMC and Frontiers full texts) returned access errors during research, so a few exact effect sizes and confidence intervals here are drawn from abstracts and search summaries rather than verified full-text PDFs. Directions and approximate magnitudes are reliable; precise figures should be double-checked against the cited primary sources before being treated as exact. Where a claim rests on coaching consensus, mechanism, or extrapolation rather than a direct trial, the text says so — that distinction is deliberate and should be preserved by anyone editing this document.

docs.title: Testing Protocol — the Week 1–2 baseline block
docs.text:
This is the gentle calibration block that turns the program's placeholder gates into real numbers. Until you run it, the progression rungs in training-system.md §7 and the readiness SWC band in §5 are guesses. This block replaces the guesses with measured starting points — without crushing a re-onboarding athlete.**

This doc is forkable and generic: it is written for "you" / "the athlete" so anyone can clone the repo and run the same block. Where it helps, it uses the running example — a fast-twitch, lower-body-dominant masters CrossFitter re-onboarding conditioning, chasing a strict HSPU and a strict ring muscle-up — but every number here is illustrative. Swap in your own and the logic holds.

Companion docs: training-system.md is the coach (this block sets its gates); science-foundations.md is the why behind every test choice; data-model.md is where the results are stored (benchmarks, benchmarkPRs); functional-design.md is how the app captures and re-tests them; nutrition.md is fueling around the harder test days; decisions.md is the log of athlete choices this block implements. The guardrails this block obeys live in CLAUDE.md.

docs.title: 0. Principles — why testing this way, this gently
docs.text:
1. The block must not become the injury. A re-onboarding masters athlete with weeks of sporadic training is at peak risk in week 1, not week 6. Tendons adapt on a ~12-week clock (see science-foundations.md §5); a true 1RM or a max-effort benchmark in week 1 is exactly the spike the whole program is built to avoid. We never take a true 1RM here, and we never test to true failure on the tissues we're trying to protect.

  2. Estimate, don't max. Strength is captured as a conservative rep-max → estimated 1RM, not a one-rep grind. The estimate is plenty accurate to set starting loads and ratios, and it costs a fraction of the fatigue and risk.

  3. Test fresh, test early, test rested. Every marker is measured after the standard 12–15 min ramp (§1), on a GREEN or high-AMBER day only, with the test placed first in the session while the CNS and tissues are fresh. Never test on a RED day — reschedule the marker, don't push through.

  4. Interleave, don't stack. The two weeks below thread the tests into normal early training so no single day is brutal. Pressing and pulling maxes never share a 24-hour window; ROM and conditioning ride the easy days.

  5. One conditioning anchor, kept sub-maximal. Conditioning is re-onboarded over 4–8 weeks (training-system.md §3.5). The benchmark here is a gentle anchor — a capped sprint set or a deliberately sub-maximal row — not a Grace/Fran for-time effort. We are marking a starting line, not racing it.

  6. Honest gaps over invented numbers. If a marker can't be measured safely or cleanly (pain, a missed GREEN day, equipment), record it as "deferred," not a fabricated value. A placeholder gate is better than a wrong number. (Mirrors the open-calibration rule in CLAUDE.md.)

  7. Spine caution is built in. With any structural spine asymmetry or back history, nothing in this block loads end-range spinal flexion. The only spine-relevant tests are unloaded ROM reaches. See §6.

docs.title: 1. The warm-up — the standard 12–15 min ramp (mandatory before every test)
docs.text:
Identical to the session ramp in training-system.md §4 — testing does not get a special warm-up, it gets the normal one, done well. Dynamic only; no static holds >30–60 s before a strength or skill test (long holds cost ~4–7.5% strength — that would corrupt the very number you're trying to measure).

[0:00–0:03]  General: bike or row, easy → moderate, to raise core temperature
[0:03–0:08]  Dynamic mobility: leg swings, hip openers, banded scap/shoulder prep,
             thoracic rotations; WRIST PREP if a false-grip test is coming
[0:08–0:12]  Movement-specific ramp: for the press, empty bar/light DB → ~40% → ~60%
             → ~75% of the expected working load, low reps, crisp
[0:12–0:15]  One or two "primer" reps at the first test load; confirm it feels easy

ROM markers are measured AFTER this ramp, not cold — you want usable, warm range, the range you'll actually train in, not a cold worst-case. The one exception is noted in §3 (re-test the same way every time, warm).
